student_message: That makes so much sense! A square has diagonal fold lines because folding it corner to corner makes two perfectly matching triangles. Why do the diagonal folds stop working if I stretch it out into a long rectangle?
final_message:
You are asking such brilliant questions! 🌟 Let's imagine folding that paper. A square has sides that are all exactly the same size. So when you fold it corner to corner, the edges hug each other perfectly. 

But when you stretch it into a long rectangle, you have two long sides and two short sides. If you try to fold it diagonally from corner to corner, the long side tries to lay right on top of the short side. Since they are different sizes, the corners will stick out and won't match up! 

A true line of symmetry needs to divide a shape into two perfect mirror images of each other. Because the edges stick out on the stretched rectangle, the mirror magic is broken. Does that help you picture why the diagonal fold stops working?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain why diagonal folds don't create symmetry in non-square rectangles.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is happy, focused, and asked a great conceptual question. The dialogue agent should explain the geometric reasoning.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: Zara covered rotational and reflectional symmetry, including vertical, horizontal, and diagonal fold lines in letters ('V', 'X') and shapes. She understands these concepts exceptionally well, accurately identifying multiple symmetry lines. Zara exhibited no struggles. Her learning state is highly engaged and progressive; having mastered basic 2D symmetry, she proactively asks advanced questions, such as how symmetry applies to 3D objects and why diagonal symmetry fails when squares are stretched into rectangles.
